fact: July 6 is exactly 187 days into the year - that's more than halfway through! If the year were a pizza, you'd have already eaten 6 and a half slices out of 12.
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is peak summer heat season when many animals take afternoon naps in whatever shade they can find - even normally active squirrels become lazy couch potatoes!
The sun rises super early around July 6 in northern places like Alaska - we're talking 4 AM sunrises that make the sky look like someone spilled orange juice across it.
July 6 falls smack in the middle of Cancer season in astrology, which means if the zodiac were a year-long road trip, we'd be cruising through the 'homebody crab' phase.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere spots, July 6 is deep winter - penguins are huddling for warmth while kids in the north are complaining it's too hot for jackets!
Many flowers like sunflowers and cosmos are just starting to show off their blooms around this date in northern climates, turning fields into nature's confetti explosion.
The Earth is actually farther from the sun around July 6 than in winter - we're about 3 million miles more distant, which seems totally backwards but it's true!
Fireflies are typically at their flashiest peak around July 6 in many temperate regions, turning backyards into natural disco parties that would make any DJ jealous.
July 6 sits exactly one month after the summer solstice, which means daylight is still super long but starting its sneaky countdown toward shorter days.
In many northern lakes and rivers, the water temperature often hits its perfect 'not-too-cold-not-too-warm' sweet spot around July 6 - like nature's own bathtub setting.
Mosquitoes are usually thriving around July 6 in most temperate places because the heat and humidity create their dream vacation resort conditions.
The moon can look extra orange or red when it rises around this time of year because summer heat creates more particles in the air that filter the light like a giant Instagram filter.
